label: Cell Assembly
definition: A group of neurons wired together by repeated co-activation into a functional unit, proposed by Donald Hebb as the brain's basic representation: a concept or percept is encoded by the coordinated firing of an entire assembly rather than by any single cell.

body.text: A cell assembly is a coalition of neurons that act as one. Donald Hebb introduced the idea in 1949 as the natural consequence of his learning rule: when a set of neurons is repeatedly activated together, the synapses among them strengthen until they form a tightly interconnected group whose members tend to fire as a unit. Once formed, the assembly behaves like a single functional element. Stimulating a fraction of its neurons can ignite the whole group through their mutual excitatory connections, so the assembly can be triggered, sustained, and recalled as a coherent pattern. Hebb proposed that such an assembly is the physical substrate of a concept, a percept, or an idea, the brain's way of representing a single thing using many cells.

The cell assembly resolves a tension at the heart of how the brain could represent anything. A representation carried by one neuron would be fragile, since that cell's death or noise would erase the concept, and it could not be partially activated or gracefully degraded. By distributing a representation across a whole assembly, the brain gains robustness and the ability to complete a pattern from partial input: present part of the cue, and the assembly's internal connectivity fills in the rest. This is a distributed code, and it is the conceptual ancestor of distributed representation in connectionism and modern neural networks, where meaning likewise lives in patterns of activity across many units rather than in any single one, a theme that recurs in accounts of neural-coding.

Mechanistically the cell assembly is the macroscopic payoff of hebbian-learning. The same fire-together-wire-together rule that strengthens an individual synapse, applied across a recurrently connected population, sculpts subsets of neurons into self-reinforcing groups, which is why assemblies and Hebbian plasticity are usually discussed together. Hebb further proposed that activity could reverberate around an assembly's recurrent loops, sustaining a pattern after the original stimulus is gone, an early and influential hypothesis for the neural basis of short-term and working-memory. Sequences of assemblies activating one another in turn, which Hebb called phase sequences, were his sketch of how trains of thought and temporally extended behavior might be strung together.

Hebb's proposal was largely theoretical when he made it, but modern recording techniques that capture many neurons at once have given it strong empirical traction, revealing groups of cells that activate together as coordinated ensembles and recur during memory and behavior. The concept remains a unifying bridge between neuroscience and artificial intelligence. Attractor networks such as the Hopfield network formalize the assembly as a stable pattern the system settles into and can recall from partial cues, recurrent and spiking-neural-network models are studied precisely for how assemblies form and propagate, and the broad lesson, that knowledge is stored in the connections that bind a population into a reusable unit, is foundational to how distributed learning systems represent the world.
